english: were released; lus dé-las (lus de las) from this body Glr.; nád-las (nad las) from disease, ṅas (ngas) by me Mil. In a specific Buddhistic sense: yid (yid), or sems rnám-par grol (sems rnam par grol) the soul or spirit is released, made free, viz. from every impediment arising from imperfect knowledge or perception, the latter being no longer subject to dimness and uncertainty, but perfectly clear; raṅ(-sar) gról-ba (rang (-sar) grol ba) what has become clear of itself (without any study or exertion) Glr.; yet raṅ gról-ba (rang grol ba) seems also to denote: to be set free, to get released (from the °Kor-ba ('khor ba)) through one's self; čos-ñid-kyi gliṅ-du °gról-ba (chos nyid kyi gling du 'grol ba) to be released and elevated into the region of the highest perception Glr.; °gról-ba ('grol ba), used absolutely, always signifies, like tár-ba (thar ba), to be released from the evil of existence. II. vb. act., pf. bkrol (bkrol), fut. dgrol (dgrol), imp. krol (khrol) (W. bkrol (bkrol), pronouneed *ṭol*) 1. to loose, untie, unbutton, unfasten, a knot, a bag, a garment; to put down, take off, arms, ornaments etc. — 2. to release, redeem, liberate, bčiṅs-pa-las (bcings pa las) from fetters Tar. — 3. to remove, do away with, put an end to, sdug-bsṅál (sdug bsngal) misery, te-tsóm (the tshom) doubts. — 4. to remove obscurities, to free from uncertainties, to explain, interpret, comment, = grél-ba ('grel ba), e.g. dgóṅs-pa (dgongs pa) the sense, import Lex.; gról-byéd (grol byed), gról(-ba)-po (grol (-ba) po) commentator Cs.

the act of going, walking, ṗyi grós-su (phyi gros su) v. sub gyés-pa (gyes pa); skyabs-°grós (skyabs 'gros) v. skyabs (skyabs); spyod-°grós (spyod 'gros) manner of walking, gait, carriage; séṅ-gei spyod-grós (seng gei spyod gros) Mil. the manner of a lion; also manner or mode of living, of transacting business, dé-tso ṅá-yi spyod-°grós yin (de tsho nga yi spyod 'gros yin) these are my occupations Mil.; mig-gi spyod-°grós (mig gi spyod 'gros) the language of the eyes, of looks; rkaṅ-grós (rkang gros) 1. a going or travelling on foot, a march. 2. breeding cattle, rkaṅ-grós spél-ba (rkang gros spel ba) to breed cattle, to be a grazier. — ču-°grós (chu 'gros) a current of water; *šiṅ ču-°grós-la kyál-če* (shing chu 'gros la kyal che) to float timber W. — °gros-čéṅ ('gros chen) amble (of a horse) Sch.

pf. rgas (rgas) 1. to be old, aged, rga-šis gzír-ba (rga shis gzir ba) to suffer under (the infirmities of) old age Zam. (cf. skyé-ba (skye ba) init.); rgas-pai stéṅ-du (rgas pai steng du) beside my being already old Dzl.; rgas-kógs (rgas khogs) v. °kógs-pa ('khogs pa). — 2. fig. to go down, to set, of the sun etc.; go-rgás (go rgas) v. go (go) 2.
a large gray species of lizard Ld.
also gaṅ-gzér-ma (gang gzer ma) hedgehog Sch., or perh. rather porcupine.

old, aged; more frq. rgád-po (rgad po) 1. an old man, a man gray with old age. 2. an elder, senior, headman of a village; rgád-mo (rgad mo) an old woman; rgan-bgrés (rgan bgres) old people Sch.; rgan-rgón gñis (rgan rgon gnyis) Sch.: 'both the old man and the old woman'(?); rgan-rgón-rnams-kyi skyo-grógs (rgan rgon rnams kyi skyo grogs) the comforter of old people (so Mil. calls himself) — rgan-byís (rgan byis) old people and children, old and young Mil. — rgan-mi-máṅ (rgan mi mang) = rgan-gsum (rgan gsum). — gan-tšír-żón-tšír (gan tshir zhon tshir) W., gen-żón-ḍhal-rím (gen zhon dhal rim) C. the privileges of seniority. — rgan-żúgs (rgan zhugs) those that are grown old Cs., 'an old monk' (?) Sch. — rgan-rábs (rgan rabs) the aged, rgan-rábs-la rim-gro byéd-pa (rgan rabs la rim gro byed pa) Stg., rgan-rígs ṗu-dúd-du kúr-ba (rgan rigs phu dud du bkur ba) S.g. to respect old age. — rgan-gsúm (rgan gsum), rgan-mi-máṅ (rgan mi mang) the elders of a village.

pf. and fut. brgal (brgal), imp. rgol (rgol), c. las (las), or accus., or la (la), to step over (a threshold) Glr.; to pass or climb over (a mountain); la brgál-bai byáṅ-ṅos (la brgal bai byang ngos) the north-side of a pass crossed Glr.; to leap over (a wall) Dzl.; to ford (a river); to travel through, to sail over, to pass (a river or lake), rgyá-mtso-la gru-gzíṅs-kyi lám-nas brgál-te (rgya mtsho la gru gzings kyi lam nas brgal te) after having crossed the sea in a ship.
old age; rgás-ka sra (rgas ka sra) a vigorous old age.
sometimes for dgu (dgu); rgu-tub (rgu thub) = dgu-tub (dgu thub) Med.; -gu-drús (-gu drus) ?
